raw_case:
First reported case of primary small cell cancer of the pancreas with positive TTF-1 tumor marker
Small cell carcinomas are very aggressive malignancies that are most often linked with lung cancer, although they may also develop in the pancreas, colon, rectum, skin, and cervix. SCC of the pancreas accounts for about 1% of these neoplasms. An 88-year-old male with several comorbidities who presented with significant weight loss was diagnosed with metastatic pancreatic neuroendocrine carcinoma after complaining of persistent epistaxis and back pain. This case underscores the significance of using atypical tumor markers, such as thyroid transcription factor 1, to diagnose small-cell pancreatic cancer. It also emphasizes the importance of a multidisciplinary, patient-centered approach to managing these aggressive tumors.

Introduction

Small cell carcinomas (SCCs) are highly aggressive malignancies primarily related to cancers of the lung, which account for 18%-20% of all cases. Unusual locations for SCCs include the pancreas, colon, rectum, skin, and cervix [1]. Small cell carcinoma of the pancreas (SCCP) is very rare, representing only 1% of small cell or neuroendocrine malignant neoplasms in this organ. SCCP is a very aggressive tumor, and hence, it has an extremely poor prognosis. Most cases of SCC of the pancreas are metastatic at the time of diagnosis, and therefore, they have very limited treatment options and poor prognosis [2].SCCP requires advanced imaging, including detailed histopathological examination and evaluation of tumor markers. Tumor markers are important not only for diagnosis but also for staging the cancer, prognosis assessment, and monitoring therapy effectiveness and remission [3].This case report presents an 88-year-old male diagnosed with an atypical form of SCCP, which was notably marked by the presence of thyroid transcription factor-1 (TTF-1)—a tumor marker predominantly associated with lung and thyroid cancers. The rarity of TTF-1 in pancreatic tumors posed a diagnostic challenge, emphasizing the complexity of using tumor markers in cancer diagnostics. Through careful assessment of imaging and biopsy, a definitive diagnosis of SCCP was confirmed, highlighting the importance of careful interpretation of tumor markers in atypical cases of small cell carcinoma.

Case presentation

An 88-year-old male with a history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, COPD, beta thalassemia minor, cholecystectomy, and appendectomy came to the emergency department with back pain and unrelieved persistent epistaxis by himself for thirty minutes. He also reported a thirty-pound weight loss over the last year, initiated after he started taking a fat burner pill. Initial emergency department assessment revealed transaminitis with an ALT of 301 U/L (normal range: 7-56 U/L) and an AST of 340 U/L (normal range: 10-40 U/L). The lipase level was significantly elevated at 1,592 U/L (normal range: 0-160 U/L). The prothrombin time (PT) was borderline elevated at 13.5 seconds (normal range: 11-13.5 seconds), and the INR was 1.2 (normal range: 0.8-1.2). Troponin was slightly elevated at 0.05 ng/mL (normal range: 0-0.04 ng/mL), and microcytic anemia. Concerns of acetaminophen overuse were suspected; however, serum levels were within normal limits. An abdominal ultrasound identified multiple liver masses, with the largest in the right hepatic lobe. A computed tomography scan of the abdomen and pelvis (CTAP) revealed a pancreatic mass with biliary obstruction and extensive metastasis involving the liver, bones, spleen, adrenal glands, and peritoneal and retroperitoneal spaces (Figs. 1 and 2).The electrocardiogram showed a right bundle branch block (RBBB). Chest CT was significant for large mediastinal lymph node conglomerates as well as right hilar adenopathy, most likely metastatic disease from primary pancreatic malignancy. Further imaging to investigate the cause of back pain revealed a T12 vertebral compression fracture and multiple radiolucent lesions indicative of metastasis—a neurosurgery consultation advised against surgical intervention.An oncology consult led to an image-guided biopsy of the right retroperitoneal mass by IR, confirming a poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma, small cell type (Fig. 3). The biopsy was positive for tumor markers AB1/AE3, CAM5.2, CK7 (focally), CEA monoclonal (focally), thyroid transcription factor-1 (TTF-1), synaptophysin, chromogranin A, and a high proliferation index with Ki-67 at 95%. CA19-9 levels were within normal limits (10 U/mL, N < 34). Follow-up labs showed increasing bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase levels.The oncology team suggested chemotherapy and a palliative hepatic stent, but the patient declined both and changed his status to DNR/DNI. Despite referrals for home hospice and visiting nurse services, he did not qualify. He could walk independently with a walker and was stable at discharge, with instructions emphasizing medication management, lifestyle changes, and follow-ups. However, within 24 hours of discharge, he returned to the ED with increased back pain after a fall. He remained stable and was discharged to a subacute rehabilitation facility for further management and support, with a focus on medication optimization and patient education.

Discussion

Small cell carcinoma of the pancreas (SCCP) is an exceedingly rare and highly aggressive tumor, accounting for approximately 1% of all primary pancreatic neoplasms [2]. The biological origins of SCCP are not well understood. Still, it is hypothesized that these cancers may originate from totipotent stem cells within the pancreatic ductal system, capable of differentiating into various endocrine cells. This hypothesis stems from a 1972 report by Patchefsky et al., which described a pancreatic "oat" cell tumor with elevated levels of urinary 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid, suggesting an islet cell origin [4].Advanced SCCP, often with diffuse metastases at diagnosis, is the usual presentation. Our 88-year-old patient complained of severe back pain and weight loss, and abdominal imaging showed diffuse metastatic disease involving multiple organs—very characteristic of this malignancy. This diffuse metastasis is characteristic of SCCP and often involves peripancreatic lymph nodes, the liver, and other distant sites. Again, in contrast to the pulmonary type, SCCP is not usually associated with paraneoplastic syndromes, as in our case [5,6].The diagnosis of SCCP is confirmed by histologic analysis of a tumor biopsy and focusing on neuroendocrine differentiation markers such as chromogranin and synaptophysin, which were both present in our patient. Other positive neuroendocrine markers included CAM5.2 and CK7. However, in light of these results, the absence of CA19-9 and other histological features determined that the mass was not of the typical pancreatic ductal origin but of SCCP arising from the islet cells.A unique feature of our case is the expression of thyroid transcription factor-1 in the tumor biopsy, a marker largely restricted to lung and thyroid cancers [7]. This is all the more interesting, with no primary pulmonary or thyroid mass in our patient. Expression of TTF-1 has also been reported in extrapulmonary organs, including the bladder, prostate, and cervix; however, it is not normally expressed in the pancreas. Incidentally, another report has circulated regarding the expression of TTF-1 in a primary tumor of the common bile duct, suggesting its more widespread expression in various malignancies [8].It is no surprise that poorly differentiated small cell carcinomas would assume atypical tumor markers. As mentioned above, these carcinomas are considered to emanate from totipotent stem cells capable of differentiating into many FW cell types. This could explain the unexpected expression of TTF-1 in some gastrointestinal small-cell cancers [9,10]. The appearance of TTF-1 in SCCP underlines the complexity of such cases and puts particular emphasis on comprehensive evaluations. Thus, the detection of TTF-1 should institute a thorough investigation rather than invasive measures next in the sequence. This ensures that the site of the primary tumor is more accurately determined, reflecting the need for holistic assessment in the management of complex cancer presentations.SCCP management is challenging because it is an extremely rare and extremely aggressive disease. As these tumors are often metastatic at presentation, there is a limited scope for treatment choices. Historically, chemotherapies like those for small cell lung cancer—including cisplatin and etoposide—have been used, with some cases responding to regimens used to treat malignant lymphoma [11,12]. More recent experiences with such immunotherapeutic agents as ipilimumab and nivolumab are promising in some cases of pancreatic SCC [13,14]. However, even with these efforts, the prognosis is still very grim, with an overall median survival of 4 months for SCCP patients who present with metastatic diseases [15].

Conclusion

Small cell carcinoma of the pancreas is an extremely rare and very aggressive malignancy that causes diagnostic difficulties and requires therapeutic efforts. This present case, therefore, supports the inclusion of atypical tumor markers, such as thyroid transcription factor 1, in the panel and the need for a proper diagnostic workup. The diagnosis also brings a ray of hope in management in this otherwise grim prognosis by being made at an early stage and accurately. It underlined the adoption of a multidisciplinary approach to management with a patient-centered focus when treatment options are limited.

Patient consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for their anonymized information to be published in this article.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 88-year-old male with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, COPD, beta-thalassemia minor, and prior cholecystectomy and appendectomy.
- Chief Complaint: Severe back pain and unrelieved persistent epistaxis lasting 30 minutes.
- History of Present Illness: 30-pound weight loss over one year, likely related to initiating a fat burner pill. Additionally, worsening back pain and metastatic disease-related symptoms.
- Past Medical History: Hypertension, diabetes mellitus, COPD, beta-thalassemia minor, prior cholecystectomy, and appendectomy.
- Personal History: None mentioned.
- Family History: None mentioned.
- Physical Examination: No specific abnormal findings mentioned, but imaging and lab work pointed to significant systemic involvement due to metastatic disease.
- Ancillary Tests: 
  1. Labs: Elevated ALT (301 U/L) and AST (340 U/L); markedly high lipase (1,592 U/L); slightly elevated troponin (0.05 ng/mL); a borderline elevated prothrombin time of 13.5 seconds; anemia observed; bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase levels progressively increasing.
  2. Imaging: Abdominal ultrasound and CT scan revealed a pancreatic mass with biliary obstruction and extensive metastasis (liver, bones, spleen, adrenal glands, peritoneum, retroperitoneum). Chest CT showed mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy, likely metastatic.
  3. Biopsy: Histopathology of a right retroperitoneal mass confirmed poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma, small cell type. Tumor markers were positive for AB1/AE3, CAM5.2, CK7, synaptophysin, chromogranin A, and thyroid transcription factor-1 (TTF-1), with a proliferation index (Ki-67) of 95%. CA19-9 levels were within normal limits.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- The patient's clinical presentation of weight loss, back pain, lipase elevation, and widespread metastasis raised concern for aggressive malignancies. Key differentials included:
  1. **Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma**: Most common pancreatic cancer, often with biliary obstruction and metastasis. However, the absence of elevated CA19-9 and neuroendocrine tumor markers argued against this diagnosis.
  2. **Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumor (NET)**: Supported by neuroendocrine marker positivity (synaptophysin, chromogranin A), but the presentation of poorly differentiated cells and widespread metastases favors small cell pancreatic carcinoma over standard NETs.
  3. **Small Cell Carcinoma of the Pancreas (SCCP)**: The biopsy revealed poorly differentiated features, neuroendocrine differentiation (synaptophysin, chromogranin), high Ki-67 index, and the unusual expression of TTF-1. SCCP is exceedingly rare but most consistent with histopathology and metastasis profile.
  4. **Metastatic Small Cell Carcinoma (e.g., Lung or Thyroid Primary)**: Favorable primary sites such as the lung were ruled out due to the absence of a primary pulmonary lesion on chest CT, despite TTF-1 positivity.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Primary small cell carcinoma of the pancreas (SCCP) with an unusual expression of thyroid transcription factor-1 (TTF-1), confirmed by biopsy and advanced imaging.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Primary small cell carcinoma of the pancreas (SCCP).

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case step by step. The patient is an 88-year-old male with multiple comorbidities. His chief complaints are severe back pain and persistent epistaxis. Let's start by listing the key points from the history and tests.

First, the history of present illness includes a 30-pound weight loss over a year, which he attributes to a fat burner pill. However, such significant weight loss in an elderly patient is concerning for malignancy. The back pain could be due to metastasis, especially with the imaging showing bone involvement. The epistaxis might relate to coagulation issues, given his elevated prothrombin time, though it's borderline. 

Looking at his past medical history, hypertension, diabetes, COPD, and beta-thalassemia minor are noted. The beta-thalassemia minor explains the anemia, but we need to consider other causes for worsening anemia, like chronic disease or blood loss. His prior surgeries (cholecystectomy and appendectomy) don't directly explain the current issues but indicate past abdominal issues.

Ancillary tests show elevated liver enzymes (ALT and AST), which could indicate liver damage from metastasis or other causes like hepatitis. The markedly high lipase suggests pancreatitis, but given the pancreatic mass found on imaging, it's more likely due to pancreatic obstruction or tumor involvement. The rising bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase point to biliary obstruction, which aligns with the pancreatic mass causing blockage.

Imaging is crucial here. The abdominal CT and ultrasound show a pancreatic mass with extensive metastases to liver, bones, spleen, adrenal glands, peritoneum, and retroperitoneum. The chest CT shows mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy, likely metastatic. The biopsy of the retroperitoneal mass confirms poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma, small cell type. The tumor markers (synaptophysin, chromogranin, TTF-1) are positive, supporting neuroendocrine origin. The Ki-67 of 95% is extremely high, indicating a very aggressive tumor, typical of small cell carcinoma.

Now, connecting the dots. The primary site is likely pancreatic, given the mass there, but small cell neuroendocrine carcinomas can arise in various sites. However, TTF-1 positivity is more commonly associated with lung or thyroid origins. Wait, but the chest CT showed mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy, which might suggest a lung primary. But the biopsy was from a retroperitoneal mass. Small cell carcinomas are aggressive and often metastatic at diagnosis. The high Ki-67 fits small cell carcinoma's rapid proliferation.

However, the CA19-9 is normal. Pancreatic adenocarcinomas often have elevated CA19-9, but neuroendocrine tumors (NETs) might not. But this is a neuroendocrine carcinoma, small cell type, which is different from typical pancreatic NETs. Small cell carcinoma is more commonly found in the lung, but extrapulmonary cases do occur, including in the pancreas. The presence of TTF-1 positivity can be seen in extrapulmonary small cell carcinomas, though it's less common. Alternatively, maybe the primary is lung, but the imaging didn't mention a lung mass. The chest CT showed lymphadenopathy but no obvious lung lesion. Hmm.

The patient's back pain is likely due to bone metastases. The epistaxis could be due to thrombocytopenia (common in advanced cancer) or coagulopathy from liver dysfunction (given elevated PT and liver enzymes). His liver enzymes are elevated, possibly from liver metastases or biliary obstruction. The high lipase and amylase would be due to pancreatic involvement or obstruction.

Putting it all together: The biopsy confirms a poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma, small cell type. The primary site is a bit ambiguous. Given the pancreatic mass and biliary obstruction, the primary could be pancreatic. However, TTF-1 positivity is more typical of lung origin. But without a lung mass noted, maybe it's a primary pancreatic small cell carcinoma. Alternatively, a metastasis from an occult lung primary. But the biopsy site is retroperitoneal, which is a common site for metastases from various primaries. 

The final diagnosis should be based on the histopathology. The markers (synaptophysin, chromogranin) confirm neuroendocrine differentiation. The small cell morphology with Ki-67 95% supports small cell carcinoma. The primary site might be pancreas given the mass there, but the presence of TTF-1 complicates. However, extrapulmonary small cell can sometimes express TTF-1. So, considering the imaging showing pancreatic mass with metastases, the diagnosis is likely primary pancreatic small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma with extensive metastases.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s severe back pain and unrelieved epistaxis, combined with a 30-pound weight loss, raise immediate concern for malignancy, particularly given imaging findings of widespread metastasis. The weight loss, though initially attributed to fat burner pills, is more plausibly explained by an aggressive neoplasm given the systemic involvement.  
<step 2> The markedly elevated lipase and imaging-confirmed pancreatic mass with biliary obstruction suggest pancreatic pathology. However, the biopsy of the retroperitoneal mass revealing poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma (small cell type) with a Ki-67 of 95% indicates a highly aggressive neuroendocrine tumor.  
<step 3> Tumor marker positivity (synaptophysin, chromogranin A, TTF-1) confirms neuroendocrine differentiation. TTF-1 positivity is classically linked to pulmonary small cell carcinoma, but extrapulmonary small cell carcinomas (e.g., pancreatic origin) can rarely express TTF-1. The absence of a lung mass on imaging and the presence of a pancreatic mass favor a pancreatic primary.  
<step 4> Extensive metastatic involvement (liver, bones, spleen, adrenal glands, etc.) aligns with the aggressive behavior of small cell carcinoma. Elevated liver enzymes and progressive biliary obstruction further support pancreaticobiliary origin. Normal CA19-9 is atypical for pancreatic adenocarcinoma but consistent with neuroendocrine carcinoma.  
<step 5> Epistaxis likely stems from coagulopathy secondary to liver dysfunction (elevated PT) or thrombocytopenia from bone marrow infiltration. Severe back pain correlates with bone metastases evident on imaging.  

### Answer:
Metastatic poorly differentiated small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of pancreatic origin.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient’s severe back pain and unrelieved epistaxis, combined with a 30-pound weight loss, raise immediate concern for malignancy, particularly given imaging findings of widespread metastasis. The weight loss, though initially attributed to fat burner pills, is more plausibly explained by an aggressive neoplasm given the systemic involvement.

The markedly elevated lipase and imaging-confirmed pancreatic mass with biliary obstruction suggest pancreatic pathology. However, the biopsy of the retroperitoneal mass revealing poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma (small cell type) with a Ki-67 of 95% indicates a highly aggressive neuroendocrine tumor.

Tumor marker positivity (synaptophysin, chromogranin A, TTF-1) confirms neuroendocrine differentiation. TTF-1 positivity is classically linked to pulmonary small cell carcinoma, but extrapulmonary small cell carcinomas (e.g., pancreatic origin) can rarely express TTF-1. The absence of a lung mass on imaging and the presence of a pancreatic mass favor a pancreatic primary.

Extensive metastatic involvement (liver, bones, spleen, adrenal glands, etc.) aligns with the aggressive behavior of small cell carcinoma. Elevated liver enzymes and progressive biliary obstruction further support pancreaticobiliary origin. Normal CA19-9 is atypical for pancreatic adenocarcinoma but consistent with neuroendocrine carcinoma.

Epistaxis likely stems from coagulopathy secondary to liver dysfunction (elevated PT) or thrombocytopenia from bone marrow infiltration. Severe back pain correlates with bone metastases evident on imaging.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: The patient's clinical presentation of weight loss, back pain, lipase elevation, and widespread metastasis raised concern for aggressive malignancies.
Key differentials included pancreatic adenocarcinoma, supported by biliary obstruction and metastasis, but ruled out by normal CA19-9 and neuroendocrine tumor markers.

Key differentials also included pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor (NET), supported by neuroendocrine marker positivity, but ruled out by poorly differentiated cells and widespread metastases favoring small cell pancreatic carcinoma.
Key differentials included small cell carcinoma of the pancreas (SCCP), supported by poorly differentiated features, neuroendocrine differentiation, high Ki-67 index, and unusual TTF-1 expression.

Key differentials included metastatic small cell carcinoma from another primary site, such as lung or thyroid, but ruled out by absence of a primary pulmonary lesion on chest CT despite TTF-1 positivity.
Final diagnosis: Primary small cell carcinoma of the pancreas (SCCP) with an unusual expression of thyroid transcription factor-1 (TTF-1), confirmed by biopsy and advanced imaging.
